content.problemStatement:
Devu has **n** weird friends. Its his birthday today, so they thought that this is the best occasion for testing their friendship with him. They put up conditions before Devu that they will break the friendship unless he gives them a grand party on their chosen day. Formally, **i**th friend will break his friendship if he does not receive a grand party on **di**th day.



Devu despite being as rich as Gatsby, is quite frugal and can give at most one grand party daily. Also, he wants to invite only one person in a party. So he just wonders what is the maximum number of friendships he can save. Please help Devu in this tough task !!
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines. The first line contains an integer n, the number of friends. The second line contains n space‑separated integers d1, d2, ..., dn, where di is the day on which the i‑th friend wants a grand party.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output a single integer on its own line – the maximum number of friendships Devu can save.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: days are {3,2}. Both are distinct, so Devu can host two parties and save both friendships.\nTest case 2: both friends want day 1, only one party can be given, so only one friendship is saved.
All three requested days are different, allowing three parties on three different days.
All friends request the same day (day 1). Only one party can be held on that day, so only one friendship can be saved.

solution.approach:
For each test case we only need to know how many different days appear in the list because Devu can host at most one party per day. The optimal strategy is to give a party to one friend for every distinct requested day. Therefore the answer is the size of the set of di values. Implementation can use a boolean array of size 101 (since di ≤ 100) or a language‑provided set data structure.
Algorithm:
1. Read T.
2. Loop over test cases:
   a. Read n.
   b. Read the n integers.
   c. Initialise a boolean array visited[101] = false and a counter cnt = 0.
   d. For each day x in the list, if visited[x] is false, set it to true and increment cnt.
   e. After processing all friends, output cnt.
The algorithm runs in O(n) per test case and uses O(101) extra memory.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * n) where n ≤ 50, so at most about 5·10^5 operations for the maximal input.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – a fixed size boolean array of 101 elements (or a set whose size never exceeds 100).

code.Python3:
import sys

def main():
    data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    T = int(next(it))
    out_lines = []
    for _ in range(T):
        n = int(next(it))
        seen = [False] * 101
        cnt = 0
        for _ in range(n):
            d = int(next(it))
            if not seen[d]:
                seen[d] = True
                cnt += 1
        out_lines.append(str(cnt))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out_lines))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
